title: First Memory Benchmark
description: Schema for sequential belief revision trajectories over persistent memory substrates.
$comment: Top-level files contain dataset metadata plus independent trajectories. A trajectory is one small temporal memory world: initial facts, later updates, and queries that test what should be believed at different times.

benchmark.$comment: Dataset-level metadata. Keep this separate from trajectory metadata so one file can hold many scenarios generated with the same assumptions.

trajectories.$comment: Each trajectory should be independently evaluable. This lets experiments sample, shuffle, filter, or ablate scenarios without cross-trajectory state.

trajectory.$comment: A single ordered belief-revision scenario. The intended flow is: load entities, load initial_facts, apply updates in timestamp order, then answer evaluation_queries.

entities.$comment: Entities give stable IDs for people, places, organizations, objects, and events. Facts refer to IDs rather than surface names so aliases do not create accidental duplicates.

initial_facts.$comment: Facts believed at the beginning of the trajectory. They are not necessarily eternal; later updates can revise, correct, confirm, or expire them.

updates.$comment: Sequential changes to memory. Updates use the same subject/predicate/object shape as facts, plus an operation and optional supersedes links.

evaluation_queries.$comment: Questions used for scoring. They explicitly encode whether the target is current belief or historical belief so models are not rewarded for collapsing time.

entity.$comment: Entity records are intentionally small. They are a namespace for references and aliases, not a full ontology.

fact.$comment: Facts use a simple subject-predicate-object triple so early prototypes can implement memory state without committing to a database or graph representation.

update.$comment: Updates are fact-like events with an operation. `supersedes` records explicit lineage, which is useful for evaluating revision and historical recall.

operation.$comment: Operation semantics are deliberately coarse for the prototype: add new information, revise a prior value, correct an error, expire a no-longer-current fact, or confirm an existing fact.

metadata.$comment: Metadata makes each claim temporally and contextually grounded. Timestamp supports ordering; scope prevents unrelated domains from overwriting each other; provenance supports future confidence/source experiments.

evaluation_query.$comment: The core benchmark distinction lives here. `query_time` is when the model is asked; `belief_time` is the time the answer should refer to.

belief_type.$comment: `current` means answer from the latest valid belief at query_time. `historical` means answer from the memory state at belief_time, even if later updates changed it.
